title: How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB): A Complete Guide
excerpt: Your Explanation of Benefits is one of the most important documents your insurer sends you — and one of the most misunderstood. Learn what every field means, how to spot insurer errors that cost you money, and how to reconcile your EOB against your actual bill.
metaDescription: Learn how to read an Explanation of Benefits (EOB), understand every field, spot overpayment errors, and reconcile your EOB with your medical bill. 2024 guide.

content.text: Most people glance at their Explanation of Benefits, assume it is a bill, and either ignore it or panic. Both reactions are costly mistakes. Your EOB is not a bill — it is a detailed statement from your insurance company showing exactly how it processed a claim, what it agreed to pay, and what portion it says is your responsibility. Understanding how to read your Explanation of Benefits is one of the most financially valuable skills a healthcare consumer can develop, because errors in EOBs — whether they favor the provider or the insurer — are remarkably common and can cost you hundreds or thousands of dollars.

This guide walks through every section of a typical EOB, explains the meaning behind each dollar figure, shows you how the numbers connect to your actual bill, and gives you a practical system for catching the errors that most patients never notice. By the end, reading an Explanation of Benefits will feel less like decoding a government form and more like reading a simple financial statement — which is exactly what it is.
What Is an Explanation of Benefits?

An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) is a document sent by your health insurance company after a medical claim is processed. It is a summary of the financial transaction between your insurer, your healthcare provider, and you. The EOB explains: what the provider charged, what your insurance company agreed was a reasonable amount, how that amount was divided among your insurer and your out-of-pocket obligations, and what (if anything) you still owe.

Critically, the EOB is not a bill. Many patients receive an EOB, see a dollar figure, and pay it — even when no bill from the provider has arrived yet. That can lead to overpaying, underpaying, or paying the wrong party. The provider's bill and the insurer's EOB are two separate documents, and they must be compared against each other before you pay anything.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Never pay a medical bill before you have received the corresponding EOB. The EOB tells you the maximum amount your insurer says you owe — paying the provider's billed amount before reconciling with your EOB can mean overpaying by hundreds of dollars.
The Anatomy of an EOB: Every Field Explained
EOB layouts vary by insurer, but every EOB contains the same core data fields. Here is what each one means and why it matters.
Billed Amount (or Submitted Charges)

The billed amount is what the provider initially charged for the service — drawn from their internal 'chargemaster' rate list. This number is almost always dramatically inflated. A hospital might bill $8,000 for a procedure that Medicare pays $1,200 for, and that your insurance pays $1,800 for. The billed amount is essentially a fictional starting point that no one — not your insurer, not Medicare, almost never a patient — actually pays at face value. Do not be alarmed by this number, but do make sure it correctly reflects the services you actually received.
Allowed Amount (or Negotiated Rate / Eligible Amount)

The allowed amount is the real number. It is the rate your insurance company has contractually negotiated with an in-network provider for that specific service. If a hospital is in your network, they have agreed to accept this amount as payment in full — no balance billing allowed. The difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount is called the contractual adjustment or write-off, and it simply disappears. You are never responsible for it.

For out-of-network providers, the 'allowed amount' may be calculated differently — often as a percentage of Medicare rates or based on 'usual, customary, and reasonable' (UCR) benchmarks, which are frequently set lower than actual market rates. This is where significant disputes and balance billing can arise. We cover this topic in depth in our guide to out-of-network charges.
Contractual Adjustment (or Plan Discount / Write-Off)

This is the difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount — money that simply disappears from your bill because of the network contract. On a $5,000 billed charge with a $1,500 allowed amount, the contractual adjustment is $3,500. Neither you nor your insurer pays this. If a provider ever tries to bill you for the contractual adjustment portion, that is called balance billing and is prohibited for in-network providers under their contract.
Deductible Applied

Your deductible is the annual amount you must pay out-of-pocket before your insurance company begins paying its share of costs. The 'deductible applied' field on your EOB shows how much of the current claim was counted toward satisfying your deductible. Early in the plan year, most claims apply toward your deductible. Once you have met your full annual deductible, this field should read $0 for the rest of the plan year.

Track your running deductible balance carefully. Your EOB should include a 'year-to-date' deductible summary, but insurers sometimes make errors in this tracking. If a claim applies more to your deductible than it should — perhaps because a prior claim was processed incorrectly — you could overpay. Cross-reference the year-to-date deductible on your EOB against your own records of payments made to providers.
Copayment and Coinsurance

A copayment is a flat dollar amount you owe per visit (e.g., $30 for a specialist visit). Coinsurance is a percentage of the allowed amount you owe after your deductible is met (e.g., 20%). The EOB will show which of these applies and the resulting dollar figure. Note that coinsurance applies to the allowed amount, not the billed amount — a detail that significantly reduces what you actually pay.
What the Plan Paid

This field shows the dollar amount your insurance company actually paid or will pay to the provider. It is calculated as the allowed amount minus your deductible, copayment, and coinsurance. Verify this number against the 'member responsibility' figure: the two should sum exactly to the allowed amount (minus the contractual adjustment). If they do not add up, there is a calculation error somewhere in the EOB.
Member Responsibility (or Patient Responsibility / Amount You May Owe)

This is the bottom line — what the EOB says you owe the provider. It is the sum of any deductible applied, your copayment, and your coinsurance. This number is the ceiling of what you should pay the provider for this claim. If the provider sends you a bill for more than this amount (excluding the contractual adjustment, which you already know you do not owe), that is a billing error or an attempted overcharge.
Reading the Remark Codes and Denial Reason Codes

Most EOBs include short codes in the 'remarks' or 'notes' column that explain how each line item was processed. These codes are standardized — the Claim Adjustment Reason Code (CARC) system and the Remittance Advice Remark Code (RARC) system are used industry-wide. Common examples include CO-45 (contractual adjustment), CO-97 (bundled service), PR-1 (deductible), and PR-2 (coinsurance). If a service was denied or not paid at the rate you expected, the remark code tells you exactly why.

When a line item shows a $0 payment alongside a remark code, it does not automatically mean you owe the full billed charge. It may mean the service was bundled into another procedure code (CO-97), that prior authorization was not obtained (which may be disputable), or that the claim was submitted with an incorrect code. Understanding remark codes is the first step to identifying whether a $0 insurance payment is legitimate or an error you can challenge.
How to Spot Overpayment Errors

Overpayment errors occur when an EOB assigns more to your financial responsibility than it should. These are disturbingly common, and they work in the insurer's favor (or sometimes the provider's) — you pay more than your plan requires. Here are the most frequent patterns.

items: Deductible applied to services that should be exempt: Many plans exempt preventive care services from the deductible. If an annual physical generates an EOB applying charges to your deductible, that may violate ACA rules requiring first-dollar coverage for preventive services.

Wrong in-network vs. out-of-network classification: If you saw an in-network provider but the EOB processed the claim at out-of-network rates — with a higher allowed amount calculation — call immediately. This can add hundreds of dollars to your responsibility.

Coordination of benefits errors: If you have two insurance plans (e.g., covered by both your employer and a spouse's employer), the primary and secondary insurers must coordinate properly. Errors here frequently result in patients being billed amounts that the secondary insurance should have covered.

Year-to-date deductible tracking errors: If your insurer's system has not correctly applied prior payments to your deductible, you may be billed as though you still owe the full deductible when you have already met it.

Coinsurance calculated on billed amount: This is a significant and more common error than most patients realize. Coinsurance should always be calculated on the allowed (negotiated) amount, never on the higher billed amount. If you suspect this error, ask the insurer to show you the calculation in writing.

Out-of-pocket maximum not applied: Once you hit your annual out-of-pocket maximum, the plan should pay 100% of covered services for the rest of the plan year. If an EOB shows member responsibility after you have reached your OOP max, that is a billing error.

content.text: How to Spot Underpayment by Your Insurer

The other direction of error is when your insurer pays less than it should — which, through the magic of balance billing or just provider billing practices, ends up costing you more. Underpayment errors are harder to detect but equally important to catch.

items: Incorrect application of a prior authorization: If prior auth was obtained and the EOB shows a denial for 'no prior authorization,' the insurer may have a system error. Pull your prior auth confirmation number and submit it with a correction request.

Wrong procedure code applied: If the claim was submitted with the wrong CPT (Current Procedural Terminology) code — either by the provider accidentally or as a result of coding — the insurer may have paid at a lower rate than applies to the actual service rendered. Compare the CPT code on the EOB against your medical records.

Bundling when services should be separate: Insurers sometimes bundle (combine under one payment) services that should be separately reimbursable. If your physician performed two distinct procedures in one visit, each with its own CPT code, and the EOB shows only one payment, check whether unbundling is appropriate.

Mental health parity violations: Under the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA), mental health and substance use disorder benefits must be no more restrictive than medical/surgical benefits. If your EOB shows lower allowed amounts, higher cost-sharing, or visit limits for mental health services compared to equivalent medical care, that is a potential parity violation.

links.text: How to read your medical bill line by line
Common hospital billing errors and how to catch them
How to dispute a medical bill with your provider or insurer

content.text: The EOB-to-Bill Reconciliation Process

Reconciling your EOB against your provider's bill is the core skill that prevents overpaying. The process is simple once you understand the structure of both documents. Follow these steps every time you receive a medical bill.

items: Wait for the EOB: Never pay a provider bill until you have received the corresponding EOB. You can call your insurer to confirm a claim is still being processed if you are worried about late payment.

Match the dates of service and procedure codes: Every line on your provider bill should have a corresponding line on the EOB. The dates of service and procedure codes (CPT codes) should match. If they do not, one or both documents may have an error.

Compare the 'member responsibility' on the EOB to the 'patient due' on the provider bill: These two numbers should match. If the provider's bill is higher than the EOB's member responsibility, ask the provider to explain the difference in writing.
Check that no contractual adjustment amounts are being billed to you: If the provider is in-network, the difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount should never appear on your bill.
Verify your deductible status: If you believe you have met your deductible but the EOB still applies charges, call the insurer and ask for a year-to-date deductible statement.

Confirm the out-of-pocket maximum: If you have had significant healthcare this year, check whether you have hit your out-of-pocket maximum. If you have, the plan should pay 100% and your EOB should reflect $0 member responsibility.
Document any discrepancies in writing: If something does not match, write to both the provider and the insurer. Keep copies of everything.

content.text: What to Do When the EOB Shows a Denial

An EOB that shows $0 paid by the insurer and a denial remark code is telling you the claim was not paid — but not necessarily that you owe the full amount. If the denial is based on a clinical or coverage reason, you have the right to appeal. If the denial is based on a technical error (wrong code, missing prior auth that was actually obtained, wrong member ID), the provider can simply resubmit the corrected claim.

Your first call should always be to the provider's billing department: 'I received an EOB showing this claim was denied for [reason]. Is this something you can correct and resubmit, or do I need to file an appeal with my insurer?' Providers have billing staff who handle resubmissions daily — many denials are resolved at this level without any formal appeal.
Keeping a Personal EOB Filing System

The patients who successfully catch and correct billing errors are the ones who keep organized records. You do not need an elaborate system — a simple folder (physical or digital) organized by year and provider will do. For each medical service, keep together: the appointment notes or discharge summary, the provider's itemized bill, the EOB from your insurer, and any correspondence about corrections or disputes. When you have these three documents side by side, errors become visible immediately.

Most insurers also offer an online member portal where you can view and download EOBs for all claims in the past 1–3 years. Set up your account and check it after every medical visit, rather than waiting for paper mail. Early detection of errors — before you have paid anything — is far easier to correct than chasing a refund after the fact.

content.text: Understanding your EOB is the foundation — but turning that knowledge into real savings requires a negotiation strategy. The Bill Cured Blueprint gives you a complete billing error checklist, word-for-word scripts for calling insurance companies and providers, and a proven framework that has helped patients cut their out-of-pocket costs by 30–80%. title: How to Appeal a Health Insurance Denial: A Step-by-Step Guide
excerpt: Insurance companies deny roughly 1 in 7 claims, but most patients never appeal — even though appeals succeed more than 40% of the time. Learn the exact steps to overturn a coverage or medical necessity denial and get the care you've already paid for.
metaDescription: Learn how to appeal a health insurance denial in 2024. Step-by-step guide covering internal appeals, external review, and ACA rights — with sample language.

content.text: A health insurance denial feels like a door slamming shut. You went to the doctor, you followed the rules, and now your insurer says it will not pay. But a denial is not the end — it is the beginning of a process that, when navigated correctly, reverses the outcome in your favor far more often than most patients realize. According to the Kaiser Family Foundation's analysis of ACA marketplace plans, insurers denied roughly 17% of in-network claims in 2021, yet fewer than 0.2% of enrollees filed a formal appeal. Of those who did appeal, about 41% won full or partial reversals at the internal appeal stage alone.

This guide will walk you through every layer of the health insurance denial appeal process — from understanding why your claim was denied, to crafting an internal appeal that speaks the language of medical reviewers, to escalating to an external independent organization if the insurer still refuses. You have federally protected rights under the Affordable Care Act, and this guide shows you exactly how to use them.
Two Types of Insurance Denials — and Why It Matters

Before you write a single word of your appeal, you need to know which category your denial falls into. Insurance companies issue two fundamentally different kinds of denials, and each one requires a different strategy.
Coverage Denials

A coverage denial means the insurer is saying the service, drug, or device is simply not covered under your plan. Common examples include experimental treatments, out-of-network providers when your plan is HMO, or a specific brand-name drug when your formulary only covers generics. Coverage denials are fundamentally contractual disputes — you are arguing about what the plan documents actually say and promise. Your appeal needs to point to the specific language in your Summary of Benefits and Coverage (SBC) or the Evidence of Coverage (EOC) that either includes the service or creates an ambiguity that should be resolved in your favor.
Medical Necessity Denials

A medical necessity denial is more common and, frankly, more winnable. Here the insurer acknowledges the service is covered in principle but claims your specific situation does not meet the clinical criteria for that service. This might happen after an MRI, a surgery, a mental health hospitalization, or an expensive infusion drug. Medical necessity denials live or die on clinical evidence — physician notes, peer-reviewed literature, and the specific clinical guidelines the insurer used to deny you.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Always request the specific clinical criteria the insurer used to deny your claim. Under ACA regulations (45 CFR 147.136), insurers must provide this information upon request. Without knowing their exact standard, you cannot mount an effective appeal.
Your ACA Appeal Rights: The Legal Foundation

The Affordable Care Act, signed into law in 2010 and upheld by the Supreme Court, fundamentally rewrote the rules for insurance appeals. Before the ACA, denial appeals were largely voluntary processes set by each insurer. Today, if you have non-grandfathered individual or small-group coverage, or coverage through most employer plans, federal law guarantees you specific rights.

items: Right to a full and fair internal appeal reviewed by someone not involved in the original denial
Right to an expedited 72-hour internal appeal for urgent or concurrent care situations
Right to a free external review by an independent organization if the internal appeal fails
Right to all documents, records, and clinical criteria used to deny your claim at no charge
Right to submit evidence and testimony, including a statement from your own physician
Right to continue receiving care during an appeal if your plan covers ongoing treatment

content.text: For employer-sponsored plans governed by ERISA, the regulatory framework overlaps but has important differences. Most large employer plans are 'self-funded,' meaning the employer bears the financial risk and the insurance company is just administering the plan. Self-funded plans are exempt from state insurance laws but must comply with federal ERISA appeal rules, which include written notice requirements and specific timelines. Know which type of plan you have — it affects which regulators you can escalate to later.
Step 1: Read the Denial Letter Carefully

Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB) and the accompanying denial letter contain critical information you need before doing anything else. Look for four specific items: the denial reason code, the clinical guideline or policy number cited, the appeal deadline, and the name and title of the person who made the denial decision.

Appeal deadlines are not suggestions. Under ACA rules, you typically have at least 180 days from the denial notice to file an internal appeal. Missing this deadline can forfeit your right to appeal entirely, so mark the date immediately. For expedited appeals of urgent care, the insurer must decide within 72 hours of receiving your appeal.

If the denial letter is vague — and many are intentionally vague — call the insurer's member services line and ask specifically: 'What clinical coverage determination guidelines did you use to make this decision, and can you send me a copy?' Write down the representative's name, the date and time of the call, and what they said. This call record becomes part of your appeal documentation.
Step 2: Gather Your Evidence

A successful health insurance denial appeal is a documentation exercise. Your goal is to create a paper trail so thorough that the appeal reviewer has no reasonable basis to uphold the denial. The evidence you need depends on your denial type, but these categories apply broadly.
Medical Records and Physician Notes

Request complete medical records from every provider involved in the care at issue. Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, you have the right to your records within 30 days. Pay particular attention to notes that document your diagnosis, the treatments already tried, why alternative treatments were insufficient, and the clinical rationale for the denied service. These notes are your primary evidence for medical necessity.
A Letter of Medical Necessity

Ask your treating physician to write a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity (LMN) specifically for the appeal. A generic form letter will not cut it. The letter needs to explain your diagnosis using ICD-10 codes, describe the clinical history and what prior treatments were attempted, cite the specific clinical guideline the insurer used, and explain point-by-point why your case meets — or why that guideline is inappropriately applied. A physician who takes 20 minutes to write a thorough LMN gives your appeal vastly better odds.
Peer-Reviewed Medical Literature

Insurance companies base their clinical criteria on guidelines from organizations like the American Medical Association, specialty medical societies, and their own proprietary Milliman Care Guidelines or InterQual criteria. Go to PubMed (free) and find 3–5 recent peer-reviewed studies supporting your treatment. Look especially for guidelines from relevant specialty societies — if your cardiologist ordered an imaging study, the American College of Cardiology's guidelines carry weight. Print the abstracts and highlight the key sentences that support your case.
Step 3: Write the Internal Appeal Letter

Your appeal letter is a business document, not a personal plea. Emotion has its place — briefly — but the body of the letter needs to be clinical, organized, and specific. Here is the structure that works.

items: Opening paragraph: State your name, member ID, claim number, date of service, and the specific denial you are appealing. Say clearly: 'I am writing to formally appeal the denial of [service] on [date], claim number [X].'
Statement of the facts: Describe your medical situation in 2–3 sentences using your diagnosis codes. Keep it clinical and precise.
The insurer's stated reason for denial: Quote it directly from the denial letter. This shows you read it carefully.
Your rebuttal, point by point: For each reason given, address it with evidence. Reference specific page numbers of attached medical records.
Clinical guideline language: If your denial cited specific criteria (e.g., 'patient must fail two prior treatments'), demonstrate that each criterion is met using physician notes and dates.
Supporting literature: Cite your peer-reviewed studies by title and publication. You do not need full citations — just enough to identify them.
Conclusion and request: State clearly what you want — approval of the claim, payment at the contracted rate, or authorization for the service.

content.text: Phrases That Work With Medical Reviewers

Medical reviewers respond to clinical specificity. These phrases signal that you understand the process and have documentation: 'Per the attached physician letter, the clinical criteria outlined in your [guideline name] are fully satisfied as follows...'; 'The treating physician has documented that [alternative treatment] was attempted from [date] to [date] without adequate clinical response, as evidenced by [specific note]...'; 'Current evidence-based clinical guidelines from [specialty society] support this treatment for patients presenting with [diagnosis]...'; 'Denial of this service would constitute a failure of the plan's duty to provide benefits consistent with generally accepted standards of medical practice under 45 CFR 147.136(b)(1).'

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Including the regulatory citation 45 CFR 147.136 in your appeal letter signals that you know your rights under federal law. Reviewers know that non-compliance with ACA appeal rules can expose the insurer to regulatory action — this phrase alone changes the tone of how your appeal is handled.
Step 4: Submit and Track Your Appeal

Submit your appeal by certified mail with return receipt requested, and also through any online portal the insurer provides. You want a paper trail proving when they received it. Keep a copy of everything you send. If you are also submitting by fax, print a confirmation sheet.

Under ACA regulations, the insurer must decide a standard internal appeal within 60 days of receiving it. For urgent care appeals, the deadline is 72 hours. If they miss these deadlines without granting your appeal, you can immediately proceed to external review — the deadline violation itself is grounds for escalation.
Step 5: External Independent Review

If your internal appeal is denied, you have the right to external review. This is a genuinely independent process — the insurer does not choose the reviewer. Under ACA rules, insurers must contract with at least two accredited Independent Review Organizations (IROs). These are clinical organizations staffed by physicians and specialists who review your case with no financial stake in the outcome.

External review decisions are binding on the insurer in most states. That means if the IRO says the claim should be paid, the insurer must pay — they cannot simply override the decision. Success rates at external review vary by type of claim, but for mental health and medical necessity denials, reversal rates frequently exceed 40%. The external review request must typically be filed within 60 days of receiving your internal appeal denial.

Request the external review by sending a written request to the insurer or, in some states, directly to the state insurance commissioner's office. Include copies of all your appeal documentation. The IRO must notify you of its decision within 60 days for standard reviews and within 72 hours for expedited urgent-care situations.
Step 6: State Insurance Commissioner Escalation

If you have a fully-insured plan (most individual marketplace plans and many small employer plans), your state insurance commissioner has regulatory authority over your insurer. Filing a complaint is free and can be done online in every state. The insurer knows that unresolved complaints create compliance risk, and many denials are quietly reversed once a state regulator is involved.

When filing with your state insurance commissioner, provide: your policy number, the insurer's name, a clear description of the denial, copies of your EOB and denial letters, and your appeal documentation. Be specific about what you want — payment of the claim, compliance with ACA appeal timelines, or both. Most states require a response from the insurer within 20–30 days of a complaint.

For ERISA self-funded employer plans, state insurance commissioners have no jurisdiction. Instead, escalate to the U.S. Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration (EBSA) at 1-866-444-3272. EBSA handles complaints about employer benefit plan violations and can open investigations into plan administrators who systematically violate ERISA appeal rights.
Keeping Records and Building Leverage

Every call you make to the insurer should be logged in a simple spreadsheet: date, time, name of representative, what was discussed, and any reference or confirmation numbers provided. If you are told something verbally that contradicts what is in writing, note the discrepancy and address it in your next correspondence. Insurers are large bureaucracies, and documentation of inconsistent representations can become valuable if you need to escalate to a bad-faith complaint or legal action.

links.text: Know your rights against surprise medical bills under the No Surprises Act
How to dispute a medical bill with your provider
How to write a medical bill appeal letter that gets results

content.text: When to Consider Professional Help

For complex cases — particularly mental health parity violations, experimental treatment denials, or denials involving very large dollar amounts — consider a patient advocate or an attorney who specializes in insurance law. Many work on contingency for significant cases, meaning no upfront cost to you. The Patient Advocate Foundation (patientadvocate.org) provides free case management services for qualifying patients. Additionally, if your denial involves a mental health or substance use disorder service, mention 'Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act' in your appeal — federal parity law requires that mental health benefits be no more restrictive than medical benefits, and violations of this law have produced large judgments against insurers.
Your Complete Appeal Checklist

items: Read the denial letter and identify the denial type (coverage or medical necessity)
Request the specific clinical criteria or policy used to deny the claim
Mark the appeal deadline on your calendar immediately
Request all relevant medical records from your providers
Ask your treating physician to write a specific, detailed Letter of Medical Necessity
Find 3–5 peer-reviewed studies supporting your treatment
Write your appeal letter using clinical language and specific regulatory citations
Submit via certified mail and online portal; keep copies of everything
If denied at internal appeal, immediately request external independent review
If insurer misses statutory timelines, note the violation in all future correspondence
File a complaint with your state insurance commissioner (fully-insured plans) or EBSA (self-funded ERISA plans) if needed

content.title: Take the Next Step With the Bill Cured Blueprint
content.text: Winning an insurance appeal is only part of the battle — once a claim is approved, you still need to make sure the resulting bill is accurate and that you're not overpaying. The Bill Cured Blueprint gives you word-for-word scripts, a complete billing error checklist, and negotiation frameworks that have helped patients reduce their out-of-pocket costs by 30–80%. Get the full guide for $49.99 at billcured.com.

title: How Medical Bills Affect Your Credit Score (2024 Rules)
excerpt: New rules have changed how medical debt impacts your credit. Learn the current regulations and how to protect your credit score.
metaDescription: Understand how medical bills affect your credit score under 2024 rules. Learn about the one-year waiting period, $500 threshold, and how to dispute errors.

content.text: Medical debt has historically been one of the biggest factors damaging Americans' credit scores. But recent changes to credit reporting rules have significantly reduced the impact of medical bills on your credit. Understanding these rules helps you protect your score.
The One-Year Waiting Period

Medical debt cannot appear on your credit report until it's been unpaid for at least one year. This gives you time to negotiate with providers, apply for financial assistance, and resolve billing disputes before any credit damage occurs.
The $500 Threshold

Medical collections under $500 are no longer included on credit reports from the three major bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion). This protects consumers from credit damage over smaller medical bills.
Paid Medical Debt Removal

When you pay a medical collection, it should be removed from your credit report. This is a major change from previous rules where paid collections could remain on your report for seven years. If a paid medical collection is still showing, dispute it.
What Still Affects Your Credit

Medical debt over $500 that remains unpaid for more than one year can still appear on your credit report and damage your score. Large medical debts in active collection can significantly impact your creditworthiness.
Checking Your Credit Report

Review your credit reports regularly at AnnualCreditReport.com for free. Look for medical collections that shouldn't be there – under $500, paid debts, or debts less than one year old. Dispute any errors you find with each credit bureau.
Protecting Your Credit During Medical Debt

Communicate with providers before bills go to collections. Request payment plans to keep accounts in good standing. Apply for financial assistance early. If you can't pay, try to negotiate before the one-year mark when it could hit your credit.
How Medical Bills Affect Your Credit Score

Medical bills do not appear on your credit report when they are first issued — only when they are sold or assigned to a collection agency and the collector reports the account to one or more credit bureaus. This means that having an unpaid medical bill is not itself a credit event. The credit impact occurs only if the bill goes to collections and the collector reports it. For many patients, the timeline is: bill issued, payment missed, account aged 90-180 days, assigned to collections, collector reports to bureaus. Understanding this timeline gives you a window to resolve the bill before credit impact occurs.

As of 2023, medical debts under $500 no longer appear on consumer credit reports, regardless of whether they are in collections. This change was implemented by the three major credit bureaus following CFPB guidance. Paid medical collections must also be removed from credit reports — if you paid a medical collection and it still appears, you can dispute it for removal. The CFPB has proposed rules that would remove all medical debt from credit reports entirely, though that rule has faced legal challenges.
Removing Medical Collections from Your Credit Report

There are three mechanisms for removing a medical collection from your credit report. First, if the debt is inaccurate — wrong amount, wrong date, wrong creditor, or a debt you already paid — dispute it directly with the credit bureaus. Under the Fair Credit Reporting Act, bureaus must investigate and remove inaccurate information within 30 days. Provide your documentation with the dispute.

Second, negotiate a "pay for delete" agreement with the collection agency. In this arrangement, you agree to pay the debt (in full or in settlement) in exchange for the collector removing the collection entry from your credit report. Not all collectors will agree to this, and the practice has become less standard since 2022, but it is still worth requesting. Get any pay-for-delete agreement in writing before making payment.

Third, wait for the collection to age off your report naturally. Collection accounts remain on your credit report for seven years from the date of first delinquency — the date you first missed a payment that led to the collection. After seven years, the collection must be removed regardless of payment status. If a collection remains after the seven-year period, dispute it with the bureaus for removal.
Disputing Medical Bills on Your Credit Report

File disputes with each bureau where the inaccurate medical collection appears — Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion each maintain their own database and must be disputed separately. File online at each bureau website, providing a clear description of the error and any supporting documentation. After 30 days, check your credit report for the resolution. If a bureau verifies the collection as accurate despite your evidence to the contrary, escalate to the CFPB with a formal complaint.

Medical billing errors sometimes carry through to collections and then to your credit report. If the original bill contained an error — wrong amount, wrong insurance processing, duplicate charge — that error may have inflated the collection balance that was reported to the bureaus. Resolving the original billing error through the provider can sometimes result in a recall of the collection account, which removes the credit report entry entirely rather than merely marking it as paid.
Protecting Your Credit While Resolving Medical Bills

The most important protective step is to request a collections hold from the billing department or collector while you are actively resolving a dispute or applying for financial assistance. A collections hold prevents the account from being reported as a new collection while the resolution process is underway. Ask for this hold explicitly and get confirmation in writing. Many hospitals and collections agencies have standard hold policies for accounts in active dispute or under financial assistance review — you just have to ask.

Monitor your credit report during the resolution process. You are entitled to a free credit report from each bureau every week at annualcreditreport.com. Check for new collection entries and dispute any that appear while a hold should be in place. Early detection of a reporting error gives you the best chance of resolving it before it affects your credit score significantly.
New CFPB Rules on Medical Debt and Credit Reporting

The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau finalized a rule in January 2025 that would remove all medical debt from credit reports entirely. The rule would prevent credit bureaus from including medical debt information in credit reports, and prevent lenders from using medical debt in credit decisions. The CFPB projected the rule would remove $49 billion in medical debt from the credit reports of 15 million Americans. However, the rule faced legal challenges and the status of its implementation should be verified at the CFPB website before making decisions based on anticipated changes.

Regardless of federal rulemaking status, several states have enacted their own protections against medical debt credit reporting. Colorado, New Mexico, Nevada, and Washington have enacted laws limiting the use of medical debt in credit decisions by lenders. Check your state insurance commissioner or attorney general website for your current state-level protections.
How a Medical Collection Affects Your Credit Score

A medical collection that appears on your credit report can reduce your credit score significantly, though the impact has decreased over recent years as credit scoring models have evolved. The FICO 9 and VantageScore 3.0 scoring models both treat paid medical collections less harshly than unpaid ones. FICO 9 ignores paid collections entirely. VantageScore 4.0 ignores all medical collections regardless of paid status. However, many lenders still use older scoring models (FICO 8 or earlier) that treat medical collections the same as other collection accounts.

The credit score impact of a medical collection typically falls between 50 and 100 points for a patient with otherwise good credit. The impact is largest in the first year and decreases over time as the collection ages. For patients with limited credit history, the proportional impact may be higher. Resolving the collection — whether through payment, settlement, or dispute — and having it removed from your report is the fastest path to credit score recovery.
Your Annual Free Credit Reports

You are entitled to a free credit report from each of the three major bureaus every week through annualcreditreport.com. This is the official federally mandated free report — not to be confused with commercial credit monitoring services that require subscriptions. Review your reports regularly to catch medical collection entries early, verify that resolved collections are properly removed, and identify any medical billing errors that have been reported to the bureaus.

When reviewing your credit report for medical collections, note the original creditor name, the reported balance, and the date of first delinquency for each entry. Compare these against your own records. Errors in any of these fields give you grounds for a dispute. Collectors sometimes report inflated balances (including fees added after purchase), incorrect delinquency dates (which affect how long the entry remains), or duplicate entries for the same debt (once from the original creditor and once from the collector).
Medical Bills and Mortgage Applications

Medical collections on your credit report can complicate mortgage applications. Many conventional loan programs require explanation letters for collection accounts above certain thresholds. FHA loans have specific requirements around unpaid collections and disputed accounts. If you are planning to apply for a mortgage within the next 12-24 months and have unresolved medical collections, address them before applying. Even a pay-for-delete resolution that removes the entry may be worth more to your mortgage application than the cost of settling the debt.

Speak with your mortgage lender about which credit scoring model they use. If the lender uses FICO 9 or VantageScore 4.0, paid medical collections have no impact. If they use FICO 8 (which most conventional lenders currently use), paid collections are still scored negatively but less severely than unpaid ones. Knowing the scoring model your lender uses helps you prioritize which medical collections to resolve before applying.
Statute of Limitations vs. Credit Reporting for Medical Bills

Do not confuse the statute of limitations on medical debt with the credit reporting period. The statute of limitations determines how long a creditor can sue you — typically three to six years from first delinquency depending on your state. The credit reporting period is seven years from first delinquency under the Fair Credit Reporting Act. A debt can be past the statute of limitations (too old to sue over) but still legally reportable on your credit report for the remainder of the seven-year period. If a medical collection appears after the statute of limitations has expired, it may still be legally on your credit report — but the creditor cannot sue you to collect it.

Medical debt credit reporting has changed more in the past three years than in the previous two decades. The $500 threshold, the one-year waiting period, and the pending CFPB rulemaking all reflect a growing recognition that medical debt is categorically different from consumer debt — it is often involuntary, unexpected, and a poor predictor of creditworthiness. The credit system is still catching up to this reality. In the meantime, patients who understand the current rules — what gets reported, when, and how to dispute it — are in a far stronger position than those who do not.

The relationship between medical debt and credit is changing faster than most patients realize. The policy environment has shifted significantly since 2022 and continues to evolve. Monitor developments at the CFPB website and your state insurance commissioner for the most current protections. In the meantime, the practical steps remain constant: review your credit report regularly, dispute inaccurate entries promptly, resolve medical collections through payment or settlement and get them removed, and stay informed about the evolving rules that determine what medical debt information lenders and landlords can legally use against you.

links.text: How to Negotiate Medical Bills in Collections
Medical Bill Statute of Limitations by State
How Long Can Medical Debt Be Collected?

content.text: Take a systematic approach: pull your free credit reports from annualcreditreport.com, identify every medical collection entry, verify each one against your own payment records and billing history, dispute inaccurate entries immediately, and resolve accurate collections through the most credit-favorable method available. The work is not glamorous, but the outcome — accurate credit reports that reflect your actual financial history rather than billing errors — is one of the highest-return personal finance tasks available.

title: Balance Billing Explained: What It Is and How to Fight It
excerpt: Balance billing can leave you with unexpected costs. Learn what balance billing is, when it's legal, and how to fight unfair charges.
metaDescription: Fight balance billing and surprise medical charges. Know when it is legal, your protections under the No Surprises Act, and exactly how to dispute these bills.

content.text: Balance billing – also called 'surprise billing' – occurs when an out-of-network provider bills you for the difference between their charge and what your insurance paid. These unexpected bills can be hundreds or thousands of dollars. Understanding when they're legal and how to fight them is essential.
What Is Balance Billing?

When you see an in-network provider, they've agreed to accept your insurance's negotiated rate as payment in full. Out-of-network providers have no such agreement. They can bill you for their full charge minus whatever insurance paid – the 'balance.'
When Balance Billing Is Prohibited

The No Surprises Act prohibits balance billing for: emergency services at any hospital, air ambulance services, non-emergency services from out-of-network providers at in-network facilities, and any situation where you didn't have a meaningful choice of provider.
When Balance Billing Is Still Allowed

Balance billing may still be legal for: ground ambulance services, care at out-of-network facilities you chose, situations where you signed consent to receive out-of-network care, and care not covered by the No Surprises Act.
How to Fight a Balance Bill

First, determine if the bill violates the No Surprises Act. Contact your insurance company – they may need to pay more. Negotiate directly with the provider for a reduced rate. File complaints with your state insurance commissioner if the bill is illegal.
State Protections

Many states have their own balance billing protections that go beyond federal law. Some states prohibit balance billing entirely for certain services. Check your state's laws – you may have additional protections.
Preventing Balance Bills

Before procedures, confirm all providers are in-network. Ask if any out-of-network providers will be involved. Get estimates in writing. For emergencies, know that you have strong federal protections against surprise bills.
What Balance Billing Is and When It Is Illegal

Balance billing occurs when an out-of-network provider bills you for the difference between their charge and the amount your insurer paid. For example, if a provider charges $500, your insurer pays $300 based on its allowed amount for that service, and the provider bills you for the remaining $200 — that $200 is the balance bill. For in-network providers, this practice is contractually prohibited: in-network providers agree to accept the negotiated rate as payment in full. For out-of-network providers, balance billing was historically common and legal.

The No Surprises Act, effective January 1, 2022, changed when balance billing is legal. For emergency services at any facility, balance billing is now prohibited regardless of the provider network status — your cost-sharing cannot exceed your in-network cost-sharing amount. For non-emergency services at in-network facilities, certain providers (such as anesthesiologists and radiologists) cannot balance bill you without your prior written consent. If you receive a balance bill and believe it violates the No Surprises Act, you have grounds to dispute it.
State Balance Billing Laws

More than 30 states have enacted their own balance billing protections, many of which predate and go beyond the No Surprises Act. States like California, New York, Texas, Florida, and Illinois have strong protections that apply to specific types of care, specific provider categories, or specific insurance plan types. State laws apply to state-regulated insurance plans (most employer-sponsored plans purchased through the state market, and most individual plans). Self-funded employer plans are governed by federal ERISA law, not state law, and the No Surprises Act is the primary federal protection.

Check your state insurance commissioner website for the specific protections in your state. If you are unsure whether your plan is state-regulated or self-funded (ERISA), look at your plan documents — self-funded plans typically include language like "this plan is self-funded and not subject to state insurance regulation." Your HR department can also confirm the plan type.
How to Dispute a Balance Bill

When you receive a balance bill that you believe is prohibited, contact your insurer first. Explain that you received an out-of-network balance bill for emergency care (or for services at an in-network facility) and ask the insurer to contact the provider directly. Insurers have contractual and regulatory leverage over providers that individual patients do not. Many balance billing disputes are resolved when the insurer engages directly with the provider.

If the insurer cannot resolve the balance bill, file a complaint with CMS at cms.gov/nosurprises. CMS investigates complaints and can take enforcement action against providers who violate the No Surprises Act. Also file a complaint with your state insurance commissioner, even if you believe your plan is ERISA-governed — state regulators may have authority over the provider directly if not the plan.
Preventing Balance Bills Before Care

The most effective protection against balance billing is verification before receiving care. Before any planned procedure, call the hospital and ask for a list of all provider groups that will participate in your care. Call each provider group and verify their network status with your specific insurance plan. If any provider is out-of-network, ask whether an in-network provider is available to perform the same service, or get a Notice and Consent form from the out-of-network provider in advance — this form, required by the No Surprises Act, must explain the out-of-network billing and require your signature.

For emergency care, pre-verification is obviously impossible. Know your rights under the No Surprises Act before you need them — emergency balance billing is prohibited, and your cost-sharing for emergency services cannot exceed your in-network cost-sharing amount regardless of which hospital or provider you used. This protection applies automatically and does not require advance action on your part.
Air Ambulance Balance Billing

Air ambulance services were among the most aggressive balance billers before the No Surprises Act. A flight that Medicare pays $3,000-$5,000 for was routinely billed at $30,000-$100,000 to patients and insurers, with the difference billed to patients as a balance. The No Surprises Act extended federal balance billing protections to air ambulance services — your cost-sharing for an air ambulance cannot exceed your in-network cost-sharing amount for the same service.

If you received an air ambulance bill after January 1, 2022 that exceeds your in-network cost-sharing, dispute it under the No Surprises Act. Contact your insurer first, then file a complaint with CMS if the insurer cannot resolve it. Air ambulance companies were given a transition period to adjust their billing practices, and enforcement has been active. Many patients who received large air ambulance bills after 2022 have successfully had them reduced to their in-network cost-sharing amount through the complaint process.
Balance Billing by Emergency Physicians

Emergency physicians are among the providers most likely to be out-of-network at in-network hospitals. Emergency physician groups often contract separately from the hospital and may not participate in the same insurance networks. Before the No Surprises Act, this created widespread balance billing of emergency patients who used in-network hospitals but received care from out-of-network physicians.

The No Surprises Act specifically prohibits emergency physician balance billing for services provided in connection with emergency care. If you received a balance bill from an emergency physician after January 2022, the bill likely violates the No Surprises Act. Contact your insurer, provide the EOB and the balance bill, and ask the insurer to engage with the emergency physician group directly. Most emergency physician billing disputes are resolved through insurer intervention.
Anesthesiologist Balance Billing

Anesthesiologists are the other major category of provider most likely to be out-of-network at in-network facilities. The No Surprises Act prohibits anesthesiologist balance billing for services connected to emergency care and for non-emergency care at in-network facilities without the patient prior informed consent. The consent process requires a specific form called a Notice and Consent — if you signed one before surgery, the anesthesiologist may legitimately bill you out-of-network. If you did not, the balance bill is likely prohibited.

Review any pre-surgical paperwork you signed. A valid Notice and Consent form is specific — it names the out-of-network provider, states that they are out-of-network, provides a cost estimate, and gets your signature acknowledging the out-of-network billing. A general financial responsibility form that mentions out-of-network care in passing does not constitute the required Notice and Consent. If you cannot find a valid form, dispute the anesthesiologist balance bill under the No Surprises Act.
Balance Billing for Facility Fees at Out-of-Network Hospitals

For non-emergency care, if you choose to receive care at an out-of-network hospital — knowing it is out-of-network — your plan may pay a reduced amount or nothing, and the hospital may balance bill you for the difference. This is generally legal for planned care where you had the ability to choose an in-network facility. The No Surprises Act does not protect patients from balance billing when they knowingly and voluntarily choose out-of-network non-emergency care.

The exception is when an in-network facility refers you to an out-of-network facility for a service not available in-network. In this case, you did not voluntarily choose out-of-network care — you were directed there by your treating provider. Document the referral and ask your insurer to process the claim as if you had received in-network care, citing the involuntary nature of the out-of-network visit. Many insurers have a process for this type of exception that results in in-network cost-sharing treatment.
Getting Balance Bill Protection in Advance

Before any planned procedure involving providers who may be out-of-network, ask each provider group to confirm their network status in writing. Ask specifically whether they will balance bill you beyond your in-network cost-sharing. If a provider says they may balance bill, ask whether an in-network provider is available for the same service. If not, ask the provider to agree in writing to accept your in-network cost-sharing as full payment. Some providers will agree to this as a patient service — particularly if the alternative is losing the case entirely to an in-network facility.

Balance billing has been one of the most financially damaging practices in American healthcare — a system where patients who used in-network facilities received out-of-network bills from providers they never chose and often never met. The No Surprises Act addressed the most common scenarios, but the protections require patient awareness to function. Know the rules, recognize when they apply, file complaints when they are violated, and verify network status before every planned procedure. The law creates the protection, but only patients who use it benefit from it.

Balance billing thrives on patient ignorance. Providers who balance bill know that most patients do not know the law, will not file complaints, and will eventually pay whatever they are billed. The No Surprises Act disrupts this dynamic by establishing enforceable rights with a federal complaint mechanism. Using those rights — recognizing a potential balance bill, contacting your insurer, and filing a complaint when the insurer cannot resolve it — is not adversarial. It is the mechanism by which the law functions. Protections that are not exercised do not protect anyone.

The No Surprises Act represents the most significant federal patient billing protection enacted since EMTALA. It does not cover every scenario — ground ambulances remain unprotected at the federal level, and planned out-of-network care is generally still balance billable — but for the most common surprise billing situations (emergency care, out-of-network providers at in-network facilities), it provides enforceable federal rights. Patients who know these rights and exercise them through the complaint process are the ones who benefit from them. The law requires patient action to produce patient outcomes.

Balance billing has historically worked because most patients do not know it is happening, do not know it may be illegal, and do not know how to dispute it. The No Surprises Act changed the legal landscape but not patient awareness. Patients who know about the protections, recognize a potential balance bill, and take the steps to dispute it fare significantly better than those who accept the bill at face value. The complaint process is not burdensome — filing a complaint with CMS takes minutes and sets an investigation in motion that the provider must respond to.

links.text: Your Rights Under the No Surprises Act
How to Write an Insurance Appeal Letter That Gets Results
Hospital Bill vs. Doctor Bill: Why You Get Multiple Bills

content.text: Every patient who files a valid complaint about a prohibited balance bill and gets it resolved contributes to a stronger enforcement environment for every patient who follows. The No Surprises Act is a federal law with meaningful penalties. Use it when it applies.

title: Surprise Medical Bills: Rights Under the No Surprises Act
excerpt: Received an unexpected out-of-network bill? Learn how the No Surprises Act protects you and how to fight back against surprise medical billing.
metaDescription: Fight surprise medical bills with the No Surprises Act. Know your rights for ER visits, out-of-network charges, and how to dispute unexpected balance bills.

content.text: A surprise medical bill can turn an already stressful healthcare situation into a financial nightmare. Surprise medical bills occur when you receive care from an out-of-network provider without knowing it – often during emergencies or when an out-of-network doctor treats you at an in-network facility. The good news is that federal law now provides strong protection against surprise medical bills through the No Surprises Act.

Understanding your rights when facing a surprise medical bill is essential for protecting yourself from unfair charges. This guide explains exactly how the No Surprises Act protects you from surprise medical bills, what situations are covered, and how to fight back if you receive a surprise medical bill that violates your rights.
What Is a Surprise Medical Bill?

A surprise medical bill typically occurs in two scenarios. First, you receive emergency care at an out-of-network hospital and get a surprise medical bill for the full out-of-network charges. Second, you go to an in-network hospital, but an out-of-network doctor (like an anesthesiologist, radiologist, or assistant surgeon) treats you, resulting in a surprise medical bill from that provider.

Before the No Surprises Act, these surprise medical bills could be devastating. Patients had no way to choose their emergency room or control which doctors were involved in their care, yet they faced surprise medical bills for thousands or even tens of thousands of dollars. The No Surprises Act fundamentally changed this landscape.
The No Surprises Act: Your Protection Against Surprise Medical Bills

The No Surprises Act took effect January 1, 2022, and provides comprehensive protection against surprise medical bills. Under this law, you cannot receive a surprise medical bill that exceeds what you would have paid for in-network care in several key situations. The No Surprises Act eliminated the most common causes of surprise medical bills.

This federal protection against surprise medical bills applies to most health insurance plans, including employer-sponsored plans, individual market plans, and FEHB plans. Understanding how the No Surprises Act prevents surprise medical bills helps you recognize when your rights have been violated.
What Types of Surprise Medical Bills Are Prohibited?

The No Surprises Act prohibits surprise medical bills in these situations: emergency services at any facility (you can only be charged in-network rates even at out-of-network hospitals), non-emergency services from out-of-network providers at in-network facilities (like anesthesiologists or radiologists), and air ambulance services from out-of-network providers. In these cases, you're protected from receiving a surprise medical bill.

For these protected services, providers cannot send you a surprise medical bill for the difference between their charges and your in-network cost-sharing amount. This protection against surprise medical bills applies automatically – you don't need to do anything to trigger it.
What's NOT Protected: When You Might Still Get a Surprise Medical Bill

Some situations are not covered by the No Surprises Act, meaning you might still receive a surprise medical bill. Ground ambulance services are notably excluded – you can still get a surprise medical bill from an out-of-network ground ambulance. Non-emergency services at out-of-network facilities are not protected. Additionally, if you voluntarily sign consent to receive out-of-network care, you may lose protection against that surprise medical bill.

Be cautious about signing any forms that waive your protection against surprise medical bills. Providers cannot require you to sign away these rights for emergency services, but they may ask you to consent to out-of-network care for non-emergency services. Read carefully before signing anything that could result in a surprise medical bill.
Good Faith Estimate Rights for Uninsured Patients

Uninsured and self-pay patients have additional protections against surprise medical bills through Good Faith Estimate requirements. Before receiving non-emergency care, you have the right to a written Good Faith Estimate of expected charges. If your final bill exceeds the Good Faith Estimate by $400 or more, you can dispute this surprise medical bill through a patient-provider dispute resolution process.

This Good Faith Estimate protection prevents surprise medical bills for uninsured patients who carefully plan their care. Always request and save your Good Faith Estimate so you can challenge any surprise medical bill that significantly exceeds the quoted amount.
How to Fight a Surprise Medical Bill

If you receive a surprise medical bill that you believe violates the No Surprises Act, take action immediately. First, review the surprise medical bill carefully to confirm it falls under protected scenarios. Contact your insurance company to report the surprise medical bill and ask them to investigate. File a complaint about the surprise medical bill with your state insurance commissioner and the federal No Surprises Help Desk.

Document everything related to your surprise medical bill, including the date of service, type of care received, facility network status, and all communications with providers and insurers. This documentation supports your dispute of the surprise medical bill. The federal government takes violations of the No Surprises Act seriously, and providers can face significant penalties for sending illegal surprise medical bills.
How to Dispute a Surprise Medical Bill Step by Step

If you receive a surprise medical bill that you believe violates the No Surprises Act, the dispute process involves several steps. First, request the itemized bill and compare the charges against your Good Faith Estimate if you received one. If the surprise bill exceeds the Good Faith Estimate by more than $400, you have the right to initiate a patient-provider dispute resolution through the federal government.

items: Request your itemized bill and Good Faith Estimate (if applicable)
Contact the provider and explain that you believe the bill violates the No Surprises Act
Request the provider's written explanation for why the out-of-network charge applies
Contact your insurance company to confirm they processed the claim correctly under No Surprises Act rules
File a complaint with your state insurance commissioner if the dispute is not resolved
File a complaint with the federal No Surprises Help Desk at 800-985-3059 if federal protections apply
If the bill exceeds your Good Faith Estimate by $400 or more, initiate formal dispute resolution through CMS

content.text: Surprise Medical Bills in Emergency vs. Non-Emergency Settings

The No Surprises Act provides the strongest protections in emergency settings. If you go to an emergency room that is covered by your insurance, you cannot be billed at out-of-network rates by any provider who treated you — even if the emergency physician or specialist is out-of-network. The out-of-network provider must be paid by your insurer at a rate determined through a federal arbitration process, and you pay only your standard in-network cost-sharing.

In non-emergency settings, the protections are similar but require notice. A provider who is out-of-network at an in-network facility must give you written notice at least 72 hours before your appointment (or on the day of scheduling for appointments less than 72 hours away) and must get your written consent before billing at out-of-network rates. If you did not receive written notice and did not provide written consent, the out-of-network billing is prohibited.
Air Ambulance Surprise Medical Bills

Air ambulance surprise medical bills were among the most egregious before the No Surprises Act took effect. Patients airlifted in emergencies routinely received bills for $30,000 to $50,000 or more from out-of-network air ambulance providers. The No Surprises Act now limits air ambulance cost-sharing to in-network levels, and the provider must seek payment from the insurer — not from the patient — for any amount above the in-network cost-sharing.

If you received an air ambulance surprise medical bill for care after January 1, 2022, review it carefully against the No Surprises Act protections. If you were billed at out-of-network rates for emergency air transport and have insurance, that billing is likely prohibited. Contact your insurer and the provider to invoke your No Surprises Act rights, and file a complaint with the federal No Surprises Help Desk if the provider does not correct the bill.
Frequently Asked Questions About Surprise Medical Bills
What is a Good Faith Estimate and how does it protect me from surprise medical bills?

A Good Faith Estimate is a written document that healthcare providers are required to give uninsured or self-pay patients before scheduled care. It shows the expected charges for the primary item or service, including anticipated charges from other providers involved in the care. If your final bill exceeds the Good Faith Estimate by $400 or more, you can dispute the bill through the federal patient-provider dispute resolution process without paying the disputed amount upfront.
Does the No Surprises Act cover dental and vision surprise medical bills?

The No Surprises Act primarily covers emergency services and non-emergency services at in-network hospitals and ambulatory surgical centers. Dental and vision care are generally not covered by the Act unless they are provided in a hospital setting. However, some states have enacted their own surprise billing protections that extend to dental and vision — check your state insurance commissioner's website for state-specific rules.
The No Surprises Act Independent Dispute Resolution Process

When you and your provider cannot agree on a payment amount for a surprise bill, the No Surprises Act provides an independent dispute resolution (IDR) process. This process is primarily designed for disputes between providers and insurers, but it affects what you pay by establishing the benchmark payment the provider receives. If you received a surprise bill and your insurer used the IDR process, ask your insurer for the outcome and whether it affects your cost-sharing obligation.

For uninsured patients who received a surprise bill, a separate patient-provider dispute resolution process exists. To use it, you must have received a Good Faith Estimate from the provider before care, and the final bill must exceed the estimate by more than $400. File your dispute through cms.gov within 120 days of receiving the bill. A certified dispute resolution entity will review the case and issue a binding determination.
Air Ambulance Bills and the No Surprises Act

Air ambulance services are one of the most common sources of surprise medical bills, with charges often ranging from $30,000 to $100,000 or more. The No Surprises Act extended federal protections to air ambulance services provided by out-of-network carriers. If you have insurance, your cost-sharing for an air ambulance cannot exceed your in-network cost-sharing amount for the same service.

If you received an air ambulance bill that you believe violates these protections, file a complaint with the Department of Transportation and with your state insurance commissioner. For uninsured patients, the Good Faith Estimate requirement and patient-provider dispute resolution process also apply to air ambulance services. The charges are negotiable and providers regularly settle air ambulance bills for significantly less than the initial invoice.
Surprise Medical Bills from Ground Ambulance Services

Ground ambulance services are not currently covered by the No Surprises Act federal protections. This gap has left many patients with large out-of-network bills from ambulance companies. Some states have enacted their own surprise billing protections that include ground ambulances. Check your state insurance commissioner website for your state-specific rights.

For ground ambulance bills, contact the billing department directly and ask about their financial assistance program. Many ambulance companies have charity care programs similar to hospitals. If the company is government-operated (municipal fire department or county EMS), ask about hardship waivers. These are less publicized but routinely available for patients who cannot pay the full amount.
How to File a Surprise Bill Complaint

If you believe a provider has violated federal surprise billing protections, file a complaint at cms.gov/nosurprises. You can also contact your state insurance commissioner if your state has additional surprise billing protections. Keep records of all communications with the provider and your insurer, including dates, names of representatives, and what was discussed.

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services investigates complaints and can take enforcement action against providers and facilities that violate the No Surprises Act. Penalties for violations include civil monetary penalties of up to $10,000 per violation. Knowing that enforcement mechanisms exist strengthens your position when disputing a bill that appears to violate the law.

links.text: Balance Billing: What It Is and When It Is Illegal
How to Write an Insurance Appeal Letter That Gets Results
Understanding Your Emergency Room Bill

content.text: The No Surprises Act changed the legal landscape for out-of-network billing, but the protections are not self-executing. You need to know the rules, recognize when they apply to your situation, and take action when a provider violates them. The complaints process exists and it works — but only for patients who use it. If you received a surprise bill that you believe violates the law, the cost of filing a complaint is low and the potential benefit is significant. The federal government took years to pass these protections. Using them when they apply is both your right and the mechanism by which the law serves its purpose.

title: EMTALA: Your Right to Emergency Care
excerpt: Federal law guarantees emergency room care regardless of your insurance or ability to pay. Know your rights under EMTALA.
metaDescription: Understand your EMTALA rights to emergency medical care. Hospitals must treat you regardless of insurance or ability to pay. Know what's required by law.

content.text: EMTALA – the Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act – is a federal law that guarantees everyone the right to emergency medical care, regardless of their ability to pay, insurance status, or citizenship. Understanding this law protects you in emergency situations.
What EMTALA Requires

Any hospital that accepts Medicare (virtually all hospitals) must provide a medical screening examination to anyone who comes to the ER, stabilize any emergency medical condition found, and not transfer or discharge patients until they're stabilized unless specific conditions are met.
Your Right to Be Screened

Hospitals cannot turn you away from the emergency room before determining if you have an emergency medical condition. They cannot ask about insurance or ability to pay before providing a screening. They cannot delay your screening to check insurance status.
What 'Stabilization' Means

If the screening reveals an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide treatment to stabilize you. This means no material deterioration is likely during transfer or discharge. For pregnant women in active labor, this includes delivery of the baby and placenta.
When Transfers Are Allowed

You can only be transferred to another facility before stabilization if: you request the transfer in writing after being informed of the risks, a physician certifies the medical benefits outweigh the risks, and the receiving facility accepts the transfer.
EMTALA Doesn't Mean Free Care

Important: EMTALA guarantees you'll receive emergency care, but it doesn't mean the care is free. You'll still receive a bill afterward. However, financial concerns cannot be used as a reason to deny you emergency treatment.
Filing an EMTALA Complaint

If you believe a hospital violated EMTALA by denying you emergency care, you can file a complaint with CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) or your state health department. Hospitals can face significant penalties for EMTALA violations.
What EMTALA Requires Hospitals to Do

The Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act (EMTALA) requires any hospital with an emergency department that accepts Medicare payments — which is virtually every hospital in the United States — to provide a medical screening examination to any patient who presents to the ED, regardless of the patient ability to pay, insurance status, or citizenship. The screening examination must be performed by a physician or qualified medical professional and must be sufficient to determine whether an emergency medical condition exists.

If the screening examination reveals an emergency medical condition, EMTALA requires the hospital to stabilize the patient before discharging or transferring them. Stabilization means providing treatment necessary to ensure that no material deterioration is likely to result from or occur during the transfer. The hospital cannot delay stabilization treatment to inquire about payment, insurance, or financial responsibility. These are not aspirational standards — they are federal legal requirements with significant penalties for violations.
EMTALA and Medical Bills

EMTALA does not prohibit hospitals from billing patients for emergency care — it only prohibits them from withholding emergency screening and stabilization based on financial factors. After you are stabilized and discharged or admitted, the hospital may bill you for the services provided. However, EMTALA also requires hospitals to apply their financial assistance policies to emergency patients in the same way they apply them to non-emergency patients. If the hospital has a charity care program, you can apply after receiving EMTALA-covered care.

Patients who believe a hospital violated EMTALA by refusing to screen or stabilize them, or by delaying treatment due to financial concerns, can file a complaint with CMS through the EMTALA complaint process. Penalties for hospitals that violate EMTALA include civil monetary penalties of up to $50,000 per violation and exclusion from Medicare participation. EMTALA violations are investigated by state survey agencies on behalf of CMS.
EMTALA Transfers and Your Rights

If a hospital transfers you to another facility while you have an unstabilized emergency medical condition, EMTALA requires the transfer to meet specific requirements. The transferring hospital must provide stabilizing treatment before transfer, the receiving hospital must have accepted the transfer, the transfer must be made with appropriate personnel and equipment, and the transferring hospital must send all relevant medical records with the patient. Transfers that fail to meet these requirements constitute EMTALA violations.

If you were transferred under circumstances that you believe violated these requirements — for example, if you were moved to a lower-level facility while your condition was not yet stabilized, or if the transfer appeared to be financially motivated — document everything you remember about the transfer circumstances and consult with a patient rights attorney. EMTALA violations can also be reported to your state health department.
Using EMTALA Rights to Protect Yourself

If you present to an emergency department in a genuine medical emergency and hospital staff ask for insurance information or payment before providing a screening examination, state clearly that you are requesting an EMTALA screening examination. You do not need to know the legal term — simply stating that you are there for a medical emergency and cannot leave without being evaluated is sufficient. The request creates a record that you presented for emergency care and can support an EMTALA complaint if care is improperly withheld.
